sanskrit_devanagari:
अविनाशि तु तद्विद्धि येन सर्वमिदं ततम् ।
विनाशमव्ययस्यास्य न कश्चित्कर्तुमर्हति ॥ १७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
avināśi tu tad viddhi yena sarvam idaṁ tatam |
vināśam avyayasyāsya na kaścit kartum arhati || 17 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.017 = MBh_06,024.017). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: That which pervades the entire body you should know to be indestructible. No one is able to destroy that imperishable soul.
That which pervades the entire body, know it to be indestructible. No one can cause the destruction of the imperishable soul.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt, ~250 chars of 4551-char full purport)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: This verse more clearly explains the real nature of the soul, which is spread all over the body. Anyone can understand what is spread all over the body: it is consciousness. Everyone is conscious of the pains and pleasures of the body in part or as a whole.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (per Advaita Ashrama edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Tu, but — this word is used for distinguishing (reality) from unreality; tat viddhi, know That; to be avinasi, indestructible, by nature not subject to destruction; what? (that) yena, by which, by which Brahman called Reality; sarvam, all; idam, this, the Universe together with space; is tatam, pervaded.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the antecedent of yena as Brahman / sat — the Reality that pervades the entire universe. The 'that' (tat) is universal, not localised to a single body. avyayasya (the imperishable) names that which has no modifications (ṣaḍ-vikāra) — no birth, growth, decay, etc. No one — neither Krishna, nor Arjuna, nor any agent at all — can destroy it, because destruction is a category that does not apply to the substrate of all categories.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Know that the self in its essential nature is imperishable. The whole of insentient matter, which is different (from the self), is pervaded by the self. Because of pervasiveness and extreme subtlety, the self cannot be destroyed; for every entity other than the self is capable of being pervaded.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the antecedent of yena as the embodied self (jīva), not the universal Brahman. The self pervades the body it animates and is imperishable on account of its extreme subtlety (atyanta-saukṣmya) — a pervaded entity cannot be cut by any pervader, and the self is the ultimate pervader of insentient matter. Where Shankara universalises tat (the imperishable Reality pervading all space), Ramanuja localises it (each jīva pervading its own body).

positions.rendering: that which pervades the entire body — the imperishable soul
that which pervades the entire body — indestructible
that = Brahman, by which = the universal Reality, sarvam idam = the entire universe
that = the jīva, by which = the embodied self pervading its own body

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both modern translators preserve ambiguity — the 'entire body' is the natural English antecedent for sarvam idam, which lets the verse read as 'soul pervades body.' Shankara reads sarvam idam as 'this entire universe' — the antecedent is Brahman pervading everything, not the jīva pervading one body. Ramanuja preserves the body-localised reading. The verse is the second place in the chapter where the Advaita / Vishishtadvaita split shows up explicitly; the engineering layer should preserve cross-tradition consensus (something pervasive is imperishable) without privileging either scope.

literal_meaning: But know that to be indestructible by which all this is pervaded; none can accomplish the destruction of this imperishable.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse names the imperishable as the substrate of pervasion — that by which everything is pervaded, and which therefore cannot be destroyed. avināśi (indestructible), avyaya (without modification), and tatam (pervaded) form the verse's grammar of substrate. All major commentators agree the verse establishes the imperishable as the ground of the perishable; they disagree on its scope (universal Brahman vs. localised jīva) but agree on the negation: no agent — not the warrior, not the weapon, not time — can destroy it. The verse anchors the doctrinal substrate the chapter has been pointing at since 2.11.
